thread.title: MDS Community Action Agency fair targets needs of growing homeless population
thread.title_full: MDS Community Action Agency fair targets needs of growing homeless population

author: @wluctv6
title: MDS Community Action Agency fair targets needs of growing homeless population
text:
MDS Community Action Agency fair targets needs of growing homeless population
ESCANABA, Mich. (WLUC) - November is National Homelessness Awareness Month and a tri-county organization hopes to bring attention to this growing population across the Upper Peninsula.
“There’s lots of places where they’re living in a car,” Menominee Delta Schoolcraft Community Action Agency Executive Director Gary Willoughby said. “They’re living on a park bench. They’re in the woods, a lot of different places in a tent. You just don’t see them, but they’re out there and they need our help.”
Willoughby says the U.P.‘s homeless population rose 18% last year. To help address those growing needs in the tri-county area, MDS CAA held a Homelessness Resource Fair today.
Willoughby says he also wants to educate the community on the diverse and complex reasons people might be homeless.
“There are so many different reasons why somebody might find themselves in a situation,” Willoughby said. “It could be a relationship ending unfortunately and suddenly. It could be a disagreement with a family member, sudden loss of a job. Somebody could be renting in a modest place that has a fire and all of a sudden, they have nowhere to go.”
Almost 30 organizations including UPCAP, Delta-Schoolcraft County Great Start, Public Health Delta Menominee Counties and Smiles on Wheels were at the fair. The Salvation Army was among those organizations.
“We definitely have seen an increase,” Case Manager Jamie Ray said. “Currently, we’re seeing about 150 families per month through our food pantry. And we’re seeing around 50 individuals for our lunch program.”
Ray says if The Salvation Army doesn’t have the resource a person or family needs, she connects them with someone who does.
“If a client comes in and we cannot assist them directly, we do try to refer them out to another local agency,” Ray said.
And if she’s unsure of the direction to send them, she has them call [211](https://mi211.org/), a 24/7 hotline whose operators have access to a statewide database with more than 27,000 assistance programs.
Willoughby says while some people aren’t ready to be helped, “you don’t stop trying to help because one out of so many aren’t successful. We’re not looking for perfection. We’re looking to try to help.”
The agency is also working on a five-year strategic plan focused on other underserved areas.
Click [here ](https://www.mdscaa.org/our-services)for MDS CAA services.
